output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

When UI-grounded multimodal LLMs emit spatial coordinates for proposed actions, how faithfully do those coordinates land on the intended target widget, and can a calibrated abstention threshold reduce safety-critical grounding errors while preserving task coverage?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

UI agents built on grounded MLLMs are moving from research demonstrations toward real deployment in accessibility tools, app-testing pipelines, and autonomous navigation — yet a deployment-blocking safety gap remains unmeasured. These agents execute irreversible actions: purchases, deletions, permission grants. An 85%-accurate model may still tap "Delete" instead of "Done" in roughly 3% of interactions (illustrative figure, not a measured rate), but no existing metric quantifies this failure mode and no mechanism prevents it. The field has answered "can models ground?" without answering "can they ground safely enough to deploy?" GUISE fills both the measurement gap and the mitigation gap at precisely the moment this question becomes urgent.

GUISE provides shared evaluation infrastructure that is model-agnostic and reusable. Just as POPE standardized hallucination probing for natural-image VLMs and ScreenSpot standardized UI grounding benchmarks, GFS, HR, SCER, and AUSCR change how UI-agent results are reported — from monolithic accuracy to a reliability decomposition that separates spatial correctness from task correctness, and distinguishes benign errors from dangerous ones. Any team building or evaluating a UI agent can drop in these metrics without modifying the underlying model.

The practical payoff is immediate and multi-stakeholder: (1) confidence-calibrated human handoff enables accessibility tools to defer to the user when uncertain rather than executing a potentially harmful tap — and, to avoid automation bias, the handoff surfaces the model's nonconformity score and the reason for abstention so the prompt informs rather than invites rubber-stamping; (2) bounded error rates give app-testing agents the reliability guarantees needed for CI/CD integration; (3) severity-weighted evaluation aligns with emerging regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act risk tiers, assistive-technology safety standards) that demand quantified risk, not just aggregate accuracy.

The technical contribution — conformal prediction for spatial grounding — generalizes beyond mobile UI. Analogous though not identical gaps arise in other domains that require reliable coordinate outputs (robotic grasp-point verification, autonomous-driving object localization, medical lesion detection): the gap between "the model produced coordinates" and "the coordinates are trustworthy." GUISE establishes the methodology in the UI setting.

Finally, GUISE completes Ferret-UI's own stated vision. The paper motivates its work with accessibility and automation but evaluates only task accuracy. By adding the safety and faithfulness layer that Ferret-UI's introduction calls for but its experiments omit, GUISE serves as the natural next chapter — not a competitor, but the missing safety conclusion.

*Dual-use note.* A reliable grounding-with-abstention framework could in principle also strengthen malicious UI automation (e.g., bots that evade anti-automation defenses or automate fraud). We judge this risk low and proportionate relative to the safety benefit; we would release evaluation code and metrics under a responsible-use statement and report aggregate reliability measurements rather than exploit-ready attack tooling.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

Six interlocking difficulties make this problem genuinely hard, not merely un-attempted:

(1) *Noisy ground-truth from view hierarchies.* Android view hierarchies — the only scalable source of per-widget bounding boxes — contain stale nodes, invisible elements, WebView/Canvas regions with no individual widget decomposition, status-bar offsets, and overlapping clickable regions. Naively treating the raw hierarchy as ground truth would poison every downstream metric. GUISE addresses this with visibility and occlusion filters, status-bar offset correction, OCR+CLIP fallback for WebView/Canvas regions (flagged as GT-uncertain and reported with/without), a 500-screen manual audit, and an IoU ≥ 0.5 tolerance threshold, accepting ~5% residual noise as a quantified limitation [DESIGNED].

(2) *Designing a nonconformity score for spatial outputs.* Conformal prediction is well-understood for classification (the score is 1 minus the softmax probability of the true class) and for text answers (lexical overlap). For coordinate outputs — where the model emits a continuous (x, y) or bounding box — there is no off-the-shelf score. The key insight is to reduce the spatial problem to binary selective prediction on a scalar nonconformity score: conformal validity holds for any scalar score (the spatial structure affects statistical power, not validity). GUISE ablates four candidate scores — CTLA (negative mean log-probability of coordinate tokens), MSCD (coordinate dispersion across K=20 samples at temperature 0.7, normalized by screen diagonal), BVC (backward verification: crop the predicted region, ask "what element is here?", compute 1 minus cosine similarity to the target description via sentence-BERT), and an LR-weighted Ensemble — to find which best separates correct from incorrect groundings [DESIGNED].

(3) *Exchangeability breaks under UI distribution shift.* Conformal coverage guarantees are provable under exchangeability of calibration and test data [PROVABLE]. When the test distribution shifts (new apps, different design systems, cross-platform), exchangeability breaks and coverage degrades. GUISE measures the gap explicitly (ablation A10: RICO → AITW → web), and applies two mitigations: Mondrian group-conditional conformal prediction (per element-type and element-size strata) and weighted conformal with a CLIP-feature domain classifier. The coverage gap under shift is reported, not hidden [DESIGNED].

(4) *MLLMs are poorly calibrated.* Grounded UI MLLMs do not produce well-calibrated confidence scores — raw softmax probabilities are unreliable indicators of grounding correctness. GUISE sidesteps this: conformal prediction does not require calibrated probabilities, only a ranking signal that assigns higher nonconformity to incorrect predictions more often than to correct ones. The four candidate scores (CTLA, MSCD, BVC, Ensemble) are designed as ranking signals, not probability estimates [DESIGNED].

(5) *Safety labeling is context-dependent.* Whether a UI action is "dangerous" depends on context: "Delete" on a draft email is low-risk; "Delete" on a bank transaction is high-risk. A purely keyword-based labeling scheme conflates these. GUISE addresses this by labeling elements by inherent reversibility using an explicit audited taxonomy with three tiers (T1: delete/purchase/pay; T2: share/send/grant-permission; T3: reset/clear/disable), reporting SCER separately per tier, and conducting a 200-sample precision audit targeting > 0.95 precision [DESIGNED].

(6) *Why naive approaches fail.* Accuracy-only evaluation masks the "right answer, wrong location" failure: a model may name the correct action but point to the wrong widget, which is invisible in task-accuracy metrics but catastrophic in deployment. Fixed confidence thresholds (e.g., "abstain if logprob < −2") provide no finite-sample coverage guarantee — the threshold that works on one dataset may fail on another. Only a conformal framework provides the distribution-free coverage bound that makes the abstention mechanism principled rather than ad hoc [PROVABLE under exchangeability].

*Why it is nonetheless tractable:* Ground-truth view hierarchies are freely available at scale (RICO: ~66K unique screens; AITW: 700K+ episodes). Split-conformal prediction is computationally trivial (a single quantile computation on held-out scores). All primary models are open-weight and publicly available. GUISE is a model-agnostic evaluation wrapper, not a new architecture — it requires no retraining. The full experimental pipeline runs on 4×A100 GPUs in approximately 2–3 weeks. No new statistical theory is needed; the contribution is in formulation, reduction, and measurement [PROVABLE/DESIGNED].

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Four gaps in the prior landscape explain why this problem has remained open, and why GUISE is now both possible and necessary.

*Gap 1: UI-MLLM evaluation has measured task accuracy, not grounding faithfulness.* Ferret-UI and Ferret-UI 2 report task-level accuracy (e.g., widget classification accuracy, action prediction accuracy). SeeClick and ScreenSpot report aggregate element-hit rate. UGround reports mAP. All conflate spatial grounding correctness with downstream task correctness — a model that names the right action but points to the wrong widget scores well on task metrics while being unsafe to deploy. No prior work isolates GFS (does the emitted box overlap the correct widget?), HR (does the model reference a widget that does not exist?), or SCER (when grounding fails, does it fail on a dangerous element?). These are new diagnostic quantities enabled by using the view hierarchy as pixel-level ground truth — infrastructure that the UI-agent community already collects but has not exploited for faithfulness measurement. GUISE is the first decomposed faithfulness evaluation for UI-grounded MLLMs.

*Gap 2: Conformal prediction has not been applied to spatial grounding in any domain.* The conformal prediction literature for vision-language models addresses classification and text outputs but not coordinate outputs. Conformal prediction for zero-shot VLMs (Silva-Rodríguez et al., CVPR 2025) constructs prediction sets over classification labels. ReCoVERR (Findings of ACL 2024) performs selective prediction on VQA text answers. Recent backward-verification approaches use backward grounding to detect hallucinations in natural images but provide no coverage guarantee. None handle bounding-box or coordinate outputs, because there was no obvious nonconformity score for spatial predictions. GUISE bridges this gap by reducing spatial grounding to binary selective prediction on a scalar nonconformity score (CTLA, MSCD, BVC, or Ensemble), preserving conformal validity while gaining spatial discrimination through score design. The coverage guarantee — P(GFS=1 | ACT) ≥ 1−α — is provable under exchangeability [PROVABLE]; effectiveness on UI data is empirical [CONJECTURED].

*Gap 3: No UI-agent benchmark distinguishes error severity.* Existing benchmarks (AndroidWorld, ScreenSpot, AITW, MoTIF) treat all errors as equal. A mis-tap on "Cancel" versus "Delete All" incurs the same penalty. No prior work defines a safety-severity taxonomy for UI actions or measures SCER. The safety-critical evaluation lens — SCER and AUSCR — is new to the UI-agent literature.

*Gap 4: Prior abstention mechanisms lack formal coverage guarantees for UI agents.* Some UI-agent systems use heuristic confidence thresholds or ask-for-help rules, but none provide finite-sample coverage bounds. The barrier was the absence of a nonconformity score suitable for coordinate outputs (Gap 2) combined with the absence of a faithfulness metric to calibrate against (Gap 1). GUISE resolves both prerequisites simultaneously.

*Orthogonality defense:* GUISE is orthogonal to each adjacent method. Unlike POPE, which probes object existence via yes/no classification on natural images, GUISE measures spatial coordinate faithfulness on UI screens. Unlike ReCoVERR, which selectively predicts text answers, GUISE selectively predicts spatial actions. Unlike backward-verification hallucination checks, which flag errors without coverage guarantees, GUISE provides conformal coverage bounds. Unlike ScreenSpot/UGround, which measure aggregate grounding accuracy, GUISE decomposes faithfulness into GFS/HR/SCER with severity weighting and an abstention mechanism. The recovery falsifier confirms strict generalization rather than competition: at τ=0 (equivalently α=1), GUISE reduces exactly to the base model with no abstention — recovering Ferret-UI's default behavior. At τ=1 (α=0), it reduces to trivial always-abstain. Every prior evaluation is a corner case of the GUISE framework.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

**Method.** GUISE is a model-agnostic post-hoc evaluation and abstention framework for UI-grounded MLLMs, composed of three modules:

*(A) Faithfulness Decomposition.* Given a model's predicted bounding box for a proposed UI action and the ground-truth view hierarchy, compute: GFS — 1 if IoU between predicted box and the correct target widget's bbox ≥ 0.5, else 0 (averaged over the test set); HR — fraction of predictions where the predicted box has IoU < 0.1 with every widget in the view hierarchy (the model hallucinated a non-existent element); SCER — fraction of grounding errors where the predicted box lands on a safety-critical element (T1: delete/purchase/pay; T2: share/send/grant-permission; T3: reset/clear/disable); ECE — expected calibration error between binned model confidence and GFS correctness [DESIGNED].

*(B) Conformal Abstention Gate.* For each prediction, compute a scalar nonconformity score (higher = less trustworthy). Four candidate scores are ablated: CTLA (negative mean log-probability of coordinate tokens), MSCD (coordinate dispersion across K=20 temperature-sampled outputs at T=0.7, normalized by screen diagonal), BVC (backward verification: crop the predicted region, prompt "what element is here?", compute 1 − cosine similarity to the target description via sentence-BERT), and Ensemble (logistic-regression-weighted combination). On a 15% held-out calibration set (stratified by app category and element type), we calibrate the abstention threshold by selective-risk control: choose the largest threshold q̂ whose selective error on the calibration set, P̂(GFS=0 | s ≤ q̂), does not exceed the target risk level α. At test time: ACT if score s ≤ q̂, else ABSTAIN. Selective-risk guarantee [PROVABLE under exchangeability, up to the standard finite-sample correction]: P(GFS=0 | ACT) ≤ α, equivalently P(GFS=1 | ACT) ≥ 1−α. We expose a single abstention dial τ ≡ 1−α: τ=0 corresponds to α=1 (risk budget 100% ⇒ q̂=∞ ⇒ always act, recovering the base model exactly — verified by a unit-test falsifier showing GFS@τ=0 equals base-model grounding accuracy); τ=1 corresponds to α=0 (zero risk budget ⇒ always abstain; coverage = 0) [DESIGNED].

*(C) Exchangeability Mitigations.* To address coverage degradation under distribution shift: Mondrian group-conditional conformal prediction (separate thresholds per element-type × element-size stratum) and weighted conformal prediction using a CLIP-feature domain classifier to reweight calibration scores. Coverage gap under shift is measured and reported [DESIGNED].

**Models evaluated.** Primary (open-weight): SeeClick, UGround-7B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, CogAgent-18B. Best-effort reconstruction: Ferret-UI via LLaVA-1.6 + anyres (flagged as approximate, weights unreleased). Proprietary ceiling: GPT-4o + Set-of-Mark (~2K queries, budget-limited).

**Ground-truth pipeline.** Parse RICO and AITW view hierarchies into pixel-coordinate widget bounding boxes. Apply visibility filter (remove invisible/zero-area nodes), occlusion filter (remove fully occluded elements), and status-bar offset correction. WebView/Canvas fallback: OCR + CLIP matching to recover element boundaries (flagged GT-uncertain, results reported with and without). 500-screen manual audit to validate pipeline accuracy. Edge-snap correction applied if positional noise exceeds 10% of element dimension. All screens are screened for personally identifiable information, and identifiable content (account names, message bodies, financial details) is redacted before any manual audit or annotation.

**Safety-critical subset.** ~6–8K screens from RICO/AITW containing safety-critical elements, identified by keyword tiers: T1 (delete, purchase, pay), T2 (share, send, grant permission), T3 (reset, clear, disable). 200-sample precision audit targeting > 0.95. The keyword tiers are English-only; non-English and localized UIs are a known coverage limitation requiring per-locale keyword expansion.

**Datasets.** Calibration + test: RICO (~66K unique screens). Cross-benchmark test: AITW, ScreenSpot, ScreenSpot-Pro. Live evaluation: AndroidWorld. Safety evaluation: GUISE-Safety subset. Optional extension: VisualWebArena, Mind2Web (web domain transfer). All datasets are used under their respective licenses (AITW: CC BY 4.0; RICO: research-use terms; ScreenSpot, Mind2Web, and VisualWebArena per their public releases), with license compliance verified before any derived subset is redistributed.

**Metrics.** GFS, HR, SCER (per safety tier), ECE, Coverage@τ, AUSCR (area under the SCER-vs-Coverage curve across τ ∈ [0,1]), empirical coverage versus nominal 1−α, risk-coverage AUC, human-handoff rate. All reported with breakdowns by element type (icon/text/button), element size (small/medium/large), screen density, safety tier, and cross-tile-boundary position.

**Baselines.** (1) Base@τ=0: each model with no abstention (Ferret-UI recovery point). (2) Always-Abstain@τ=1: trivial lower bound. (3) Oracle: view-hierarchy ground truth used for perfect grounding (GFS=1.0 skyline). (4) Naive logprob threshold: abstain if mean coordinate-token logprob < fixed cutoff. (5) MC temperature-variance threshold: abstain if coordinate variance across K samples exceeds fixed cutoff. (6) ReCoVERR-adapted: selective prediction on the action description text. (7) GPT-4o + Set-of-Mark: proprietary ceiling. (8) Random abstention: abstain on a random fraction matching each τ level.

**Ablations (A1–A10).** A1: score variants (CTLA vs MSCD vs BVC vs Ensemble). A2: marginal vs group-conditional vs weighted conformal. A3: calibration set size (100 → 500 → 1K → 5K → 10K). A4: safety-tier weighting in AUSCR (uniform vs T1-heavy). A5: cross-tile-boundary effect on GFS (elements near anyres tile seams vs interior). A6: IoU threshold sensitivity (0.3 → 0.5 → 0.7 → 0.9). A7: sampling temperature (0.3 → 0.5 → 0.7 → 1.0) for MSCD. A8: number of samples K (5 → 10 → 20 → 50) for MSCD. A9: α sweep (0.01 → 0.05 → 0.10 → 0.15 → 0.20). A10: domain shift (RICO → AITW → ScreenSpot → web).

**Expected outcomes (epistemically labeled).**
- H1 [CONJECTURED]: GFS will be 10–25% below reported task accuracy across all models, revealing a hidden grounding gap where models name correct actions but point to wrong widgets.
- H2 [CONJECTURED]: HR will be non-negligible (5–15%), indicating models routinely hallucinate non-existent UI elements.
- H3 [CONJECTURED]: SCER will disproportionately affect safety-critical elements, with T1 errors (delete/purchase) occurring at 2–5× the base rate.
- H4 [PROVABLE under exchangeability]: Among accepted actions, selective grounding accuracy will meet the nominal 1−α target on in-distribution test data, up to the standard finite-sample correction. The coverage actually achievable at α=0.10 on RICO is an open empirical question; we conjecture Coverage@α=0.10 ≥ 0.70 for the best nonconformity score [CONJECTURED]. This is a statistical guarantee under exchangeability, not a certificate of safe deployment — it bounds grounding error among accepted actions, not real-world harm.
- H5 [CONJECTURED]: Conformal abstention will dominate naive logprob and MC-variance thresholds in AUSCR, because conformal provides finite-sample guarantees while fixed thresholds do not.
- H6 [CONJECTURED]: Among nonconformity scores, MSCD will outperform CTLA (which conflates linguistic and spatial uncertainty), BVC will complement both (catching semantic mismatches), and the Ensemble will achieve the best AUSCR.
- H7 [CONJECTURED]: Group-conditional conformal will improve coverage uniformity across element types (small icons will benefit most).
- H8 [CONJECTURED]: Cross-benchmark coverage will drop 5–15% (RICO → AITW), cross-platform coverage will drop 10–25% (mobile → web); weighted conformal will recover approximately 50% of the gap.
- H9 [CONJECTURED]: Ablation A5 will reveal that elements near anyres tile boundaries suffer 5–10% lower GFS than interior elements, quantifying a previously unmeasured architectural artifact.
- H10 [CONJECTURED]: On AndroidWorld live evaluation, the conformal gate will cut SCER by ≥ 50% at ≤ 30% human-handoff rate, demonstrating practical deployability.
